turns.content: I need to vent about something, and I would appreciate a patient ear even if the subject is not exactly novel.
Go ahead — sometimes you just need to get it out. What's on your mind?

Standardized testing. The entire apparatus. I have spent the better part of this week rearranging my curriculum to accommodate yet another round of state-mandated testing that has nothing to do with what I teach, and I am struggling to see how any of this serves the students it claims to serve.

That sounds genuinely frustrating — losing instructional time to assessments that don't align with your curriculum. What's the specific disruption this time? Is it schedule changes, lost class periods, or something else?

All of it. We lose three days of instruction for state testing. Three days. In April, which is already the most compressed month of the academic year because of spring break and various administrative diversions. My students are pulled out of class, the schedule is reorganized into a Kafkaesque arrangement that bears no resemblance to our normal day, and I am expected to maintain instructional momentum as though nothing has happened. Meanwhile, the results of these tests will arrive sometime in July, at which point they will be used to generate a number that ostensibly measures my effectiveness as a teacher. The circularity of it would be comic if it were not so consequential.

That's a lot of valid frustrations layered together. The timing issue alone — pulling students out during the most critical stretch of the year — seems like it actively works against the educational outcomes it's supposed to measure. And the feedback delay is a real problem. Assessment data that arrives months after instruction is useless for actually adjusting your teaching. It's purely evaluative at that point, not formative.

Precisely. And that is the fundamental contradiction that no one in the testing-industrial complex wants to acknowledge. If the purpose of assessment is to improve instruction, then the assessment needs to be timely, relevant, and actionable. State standardized tests are none of those things. They are a blunt instrument designed to generate data for policymakers and newspaper rankings, and they have been dressed up in the language of educational accountability to make them palatable. I have watched this machinery grow for over two decades. No Child Left Behind, Race to the Top, Every Student Succeeds Act — the names change, the rhetorical framing shifts, but the fundamental assumption remains the same: that learning can be meaningfully reduced to a single metric, administered on a single day, under artificial conditions.

You're touching on something that a lot of educators feel deeply but struggle to articulate as clearly as you just did. The gap between what standardized tests actually measure and what they claim to measure is well-documented in educational research. They tend to be better at measuring socioeconomic background and test-taking ability than genuine understanding or critical thinking.

And you're right that the policy landscape has basically recycled the same core idea under different branding for decades. The assumption that external, high-stakes accountability will drive improvement has a very mixed track record at best.

Does your school or district give you any meaningful way to push back on this, or is it just something you absorb and work around?

Work around. That is exactly the right phrase, because it captures the absurdity perfectly. We do not engage with standardized testing as an educational practice. We work around it, the way you would work around a sinkhole in the middle of the road. There are committees, yes. There are feedback forms. There is a district testing coordinator whose job, as far as I can tell, consists primarily of sending emails that begin with the phrase 'As a reminder.' But none of these mechanisms result in substantive change, because the decisions are not made at the district level. They are made at the state level, by people who have never managed a classroom during testing week.

The sinkhole analogy is apt. And the disconnect between who designs these policies and who lives with their consequences is one of the most persistent problems in education policy. The people writing the mandates rarely experience the downstream effects — the lost instructional time, the stress on students, the bureaucratic contortions teachers have to perform.

Do you find that the testing disruption affects your students' attitudes too, or is this mainly a structural and scheduling frustration for you as a teacher?

Oh, the students feel it acutely. There is a particular brand of demoralization that settles over a classroom when students are asked to take a test that they know does not count toward their grade, does not reflect what they have been learning, and exists primarily to evaluate their school rather than to help them individually. They are not stupid. They understand the transaction. And the message it sends is corrosive: your time is not your own, your learning is secondary to our data needs, and the adults in charge of your education prioritize metrics over meaning. I have students who sit through these tests with visible resentment, and I honestly cannot fault them for it.

That's a really important point. Students pick up on institutional priorities faster than most adults give them credit for. When they sense that an assessment isn't really for them — that they're providing data for someone else's purposes — it erodes trust in the whole educational enterprise. And that erosion is much harder to measure than a test score, but arguably more damaging in the long run.

It sounds like you're carrying a lot of frustration that goes beyond just this week's scheduling disruption. This is about the larger system and what it communicates about the values of education.

It is. And I recognize that I am not saying anything that has not been said by thousands of teachers before me, many of them far more eloquently. But there is something particularly galling about watching this play out year after year, decade after decade, knowing that the evidence against the effectiveness of high-stakes standardized testing is overwhelming, and watching the system continue unchanged. It reminds me of nothing so much as the ancien regime — a structure so invested in its own perpetuation that it cannot process information that threatens its legitimacy. The testing apparatus has become an institution that exists primarily to justify its own existence.

That's a sharp historical parallel — an entrenched system that has become self-justifying and resistant to the very evidence it claims to value. And like the ancien regime, it's sustained by a network of interests — testing companies, policy consultants, accountability frameworks — that all depend on the current structure continuing.

For what it's worth, I think the fact that you're still this passionate about it after all these years of teaching is significant. Plenty of people in your position would have stopped caring by now. The frustration comes from actually believing that education should be something better than what the testing regime reduces it to.

That is a generous interpretation of what may simply be stubbornness. But I appreciate the patience. I did warn you that the subject was not novel. I feel somewhat better having said all of that out loud, even if nothing will change on Monday morning.

Sometimes articulating the frustration clearly is its own form of relief, even when the system stays the same. And stubbornness in defense of good education is probably a virtue. I hope the rest of testing week goes as smoothly as it can, given the circumstances.
